column1: Sentence
BACKDOOR
OPERATOR'S MANUAL
Backdoor is a bot with an emphasis on stealth and lightweight:
- lightweight: nothing superfluous, only the bare minimum of functionality needed
- two-stage loading: loader (1st stage), functionality in the main bot (2nd stage)
- possibility of automatic and manual fixing in the system
- only the bootloader is saved on the hard disk, all AB detections are dumped to it
- redundant communication with C&C servers:
- hardwired shim list
- backup Emercoin domains
- Emercoin domains generation by date
- server validation by crypto-key (protection against control hijacking by domain hijacking)
- possibility of automatic loading of the required bit size (x86->x64)
- piece-by-piece update of only individual bots
- stealth: minimize disk writes during any operation
- antivirus bypass by removing hooks
- regular cleaning
Bot functionality is minimal, but sufficient to run third-party tools.
You can:
- get system information
- run .exe
- run .dll via rundll32
- run .bat-files and execute individual commands via cmd.exe
- Run .ps1 files and execute individual PowerShell commands
- execute shell code
- download an arbitrary file (10M size limit)
- kill a process
- uninstall from disk
The bot does NOT include any password stylers, injectors, grabbers, scanners, horizontal distribution, sploits and other tools.
You use either third-party vendors, or if necessary, we provide our own, executed as a separate software.
For the emphasis on lightweighting.
Control is done via the operator panel.
RECOMMENDED TACTICS
The backdoor is intended to be used as a command shell.
It is not only a launcher for your Cobalt Strike session (although that is how the backdoor is mostly used).
You can do the main thing that post-exploitation frameworks allow - execute ANY commands.
Both those already existing on the target machine (living off the land tactic),
or your private tools loaded through the admin on the target.
In cases where the framework session cannot be raised, it is still possible to work off the target using your skill.
Any third-party tools must be formatted as .exe or .dll
with a compatible entry point.
The authors are willing to facilitate backdoor compatibility testing of tools in any way possible, but the initiative should come from the pen tester.
By default, after the first launch, the bot performs minimal activity - it does not try to get information about the system, it does not try to anchor (if it is built as a non-resident bot).
All these activities are dangerous.
To reduce the probability of detection, enough time should pass between the bot's start and these actions.
Nevertheless, you can order a resident loader (with automatic fixing),
or you can use the autorun commands function to automate these actions (see the corresponding section).
For example, you can write a pause after startup of 1 hour, execution of SysInfo command, pause, execution of Anchor command into autorun.
The uniqueness of pauses/sequence at each run will complicate behavioral analysis for AB/Yara.
For each new upload a new set of binaries should be created, changing the traffic profile and encryption keys (see the corresponding section),
thus ensuring the uniqueness of the format of network requests and responses to the command server.
Attention should be paid to cloaking (screening out research requests) - see the corresponding section.
Honeypots that are in the Honeypots section get 500 responses to any requests.
The bot tries to minimize disk activity as much as possible by implementing stateless behavior.
That is, not storing any configs anywhere on disk (the C&C and group addresses are hardwired into itself),
not storing its identifier (it is always generated the same for the same computer).
As a consequence, its lifetime is limited by the lifetime of pads (reverse proxies protecting C&C servers).
As a rule it is weeks, sometimes months, in the worst case - a day.
In case the pads die off, there is a cold start procedure where the bot uses the DGA algorithm (domain name generation) to search for C&C addresses.
In case of necessity to raise bots dead due to gaskets, system administrators should be notified to register backup domains.
The function of bot updating is provided for:
- replacing the list of gaskets in the bot;
- cleaning from AB detections.
To update the bot you need to:
- order from the coder a set of binaries with the changed version
- give the update command "Force update now" on the required bots, specifying the new binary of the locker.
Choose the correct timeout parameter when executing commands/running files.
If you are bringing up a session of another framework, unloading a bot, in a word - starting a process that will run for a long time (forever), choose "Background run".
If you choose any other timeout, your command must have time to complete before it expires.
Otherwise, the command will be terminated by the bot forcibly - for you it will look like "I start a session, it runs for 5 minutes and tears up",
or "I upload another bot, it runs for 5 minutes and gets nailed".
In case your load doesn't start but the bot survives, do some root cause analysis.
Check AB response, read logs.
Windows Defender log can be read like this (powershell):
Get-WinEvent -LogName "Microsoft-Windows-Windows Defender/Operational"
Where-Object {
$_.TimeCreated -ge
$_.Id
OPERATOR PANEL
Login via tor domain.
There are 4 bot registries in the menu:
- Bots - important bots (true bots)
- Unimportant - true bots that have no special value (Workgroups, etc.).
- Honeypots - Honeypots, cloud ABs, explorer machines
- Test Bots - bots on which testing is performed
On the right side there is an icon |> - this is a jump to information to the bot card.
Bot registries have a mechanism for group execution of commands and transferring bots between registries.
There are sections on the bot page:
- command builder
- bot info
- command history
COMMAND EXECUTION
The bot spins in a loop waiting for a command.
There is an interval between "give command" requests to the server.
By default, a random interval of 30 seconds to 2 minutes is used - when there are no commands from the operator - this is the hibernation mode.
After the operator enters a command, the bot comes out of hibernation mode.
The maximum request interval is equal to the timeout value in the command builder for the selected command.
It should be understood that the result of command execution may come before the command timeout expires - and then the actual response time of the bot will be shorter.
This is done to make inactive bots less visible in network traffic.
The price for this is that sometimes you have to wait.
Once again, simply put:
- while there are no commands, we are in a 5-minute cycle of requesting commands;
- when there are commands, the speed of tapping depends on the timeout of the last command.
Most commands are blocking: that is, you can't execute the next command without waiting for a response from the previous one.
In case the bot hangs up and there is no response, there is an option to reset the command (see below).
Further details about command settings.
NO OPERATION
This is a way to change the command request interval, by the specified amount of time.
In this case, the bot chooses a random interval before the next suck in the range from single to double the timeout specified in the command.
It's also a way to ping the bot.
Doesn't do anything else.
SYSTEM INFO.
Gets information about the system and sends it to the server.
By default, the bot does not do this when it enters the system, so as not to shine
(requests for such information can be behavioral triggers).
The composition and format of information about the system is set rigidly at the bot level.
SYSINFO.BAT
Also receives information about the system.
Unlike the "System Info" command, the data is obtained using an external bat-script.
The composition and format of this information is determined by the operator's preferences.
The text of the script is given in the appendix.
RUN .EXE
Launch .exe-file for execution.
Upload the file through the admin area (or select a previously uploaded file from the history).
Further nuances:
* to run successfully, the file must be statically linked (i.e., it must not drag a bunch of .dlls behind it).
for most hacker tools this is the case
* select the startup type:
- Process Hollowing - fileless startup #1 (preferred option, we choose it when we don't know what we're doing)
- Process Doppelganging - fileless startup #2 (doesn't work in recent versions of Windows 10)
- CreateProcess - startup with resetting the file to disk.
Use it when there is no other way or it is safe (no AV, etc.).
The file is naturally cleaned up after execution
For fileless startup, the file MUST be of the same bit size as the bot itself (or as the target OS bit size for the mode when auto-loading is used).
This restriction will be removed in future versions.
* timeout
- background run - start a command in the background and immediately switch to waiting for the next command.
This is how we run background services, daemons, etc. etc.
The output of the command is not registered and the bot is not interested in its fate.
- other timeout values - if the command is not completed during this time, we fix the error and proceed to the request for the next command.
Do not accept any other commands (except Reset) until the command is completed.
Here and further, for all interactive startup commands, the output of the command is logged (stdout and stderr).
If the command completes by timeout, the
- the process is terminated forcibly and marked as terminated
- The process output (stdout/stderr) will be passed to the admin area on the basis of the "as long as we had time" principle.
All files of tracked processes are deleted after the process is terminated (either normally or by timeout).
For untracked processes (background run) files are not touched.
* Host Process
For fileless running (Process Hollowing/Process Doppelganging) we need to select the zombie process we are going to work under the guise of.
Select the process so that for console applications the host process is also console (cmd.exe),
for window applications (built with the /SUBSYSTEM Windows switch) it is indifferent (there is no such restriction).
This field is irrelevant for CreateProcess startup.
* Select/Upload execution file
Everything is clear here.
Uploaded files are saved in the history.
* Script params/PID/File name
Command line.
RUN .DLL
Run a function from the selected .dll using the rundll32.exe utility.
All parameters are the same as in Run .exe; in the "Script params/PID/File name" field enter the name and arguments of the function to be executed from the .dll.
For example, if you enter
Start 1 2 3 4
then the Start() function existing in .dll
will be executed and parameters 1, 2, 3, 4 will be passed to it.
The function must be formatted as
void CALLBACK NameFuction(HWND hwnd, HINSTANCE hinst, LPSTR lpszCmdLine, int nCmdShow);
From wikipedia:
Typical error sources
The function called by Rundll32.exe is assumed to have the following signature[6]:
void CALLBACK NameFuction(HWND hwnd, HINSTANCE hinst, LPSTR lpszCmdLine, int nCmdShow);
This, however, is often neglected[7] (such examples are on this page).
This often leads to stack corruption[8] and unpredictable behavior such as looping.
RUN .BAT
All parameters as described before, except;
Run Type
- Run From Memory - run fileless (from memory)
- Dump And Run - run with dumping the script to disk and deleting it after execution.
* Script params/PID/File name - you can load the script as a file.
or
* Script - you can type the script by hand (the field is resized, grab with the mouse at the bottom right).
When running from memory, each line of the script must end with \r\n.
When running from disk, this is not critical.
RUN POWERSHELL
It's just like Run .bat.
The script is run like this:
powershell -executionpolicy bypass -file tmpscript.ps1 %cmdline%
RESET
Used to reset a hung command.
Tells the bot to cancel the previous command, if there was one.
FORCE UPDATE NOW
Updating the loader.
In the command select a new file - the bot downloads and drops it to disk instead of the old bootloader file, then runs it.
RUN SHELL CODE
Execute the given shell code within the current process.
RUN WITH EXTREME CAUTION!
Shell code is specified in 16-character representation, in any of the possible formats, i.e.:
aa bb cc dd ee ff .
aa bbcc dd eeff ...
aabbccddeeff ...
\xAA, \xBB, \xCC, \xDD, ...
Character case is ignored.
Shell code CANNOT be checked before starting!
The probability of a bot crashing is high!
This is a last resort.
You must be sure of what you are doing.
It is recommended to test on a test bot first.
TERMINATE PROCESS
Kill an arbitrary process (if it has enough permissions).
In Script params/PID/File name enter the PID of the process.
ANCHOR
Command the bot to "anchor to the system".
It is used for assemblies with manual mode of anchoring.
If necessary, you can use the manual mode for anchoring instead of the automatic mode, and only the required bots can be anchored in the system.
DOWNLOAD FILE
Request to download an arbitrary file from disk.
In the Script params/PID/File name field enter the FULL PATH to the file.
The size limit is 10M (for now).
It is necessary for secrecy mainly, but also because it requires significant hard disk resources on the server.
SUICIDE
Removes the bootloader and all traces of activity from the disk.
The bot itself is not saved to disk in any form.
DASHBOARD
The dashboard displays statistics on bots, broken down by groups and sections (registries) of bots.
GROUPS
Groups are used to separate botnets of different clients, as well as for logical organization of bots.
The group is flashed in the bot and cannot be changed under any conditions.
The group is fixed in the database when the bot is first launched and is further bound to the bot ID.
BOT TRAFFIC PROFILES
To complicate bot detection by its traffic, it is possible to change the type of HTTP requests for communication with C&C:
- add false cookies to add uniqueness to requests
- change URI suffixes for exchange with C&C
- set RSA encryption keys to encrypt the exchange.
Profiles are changed on a group by group basis.
COMMAND AUTOLOADING
It is possible to automate the processing of new bots - form a sequence of commands that will be added to the command queue of first-time bots.
The "autoload packages" functionality is implemented for this purpose.
Groups can be divided by the way of processing incoming bots, and a different sequence of commands can be set for each group.
CLOAKING
It is possible to automatically filter bots by countries and/or address ranges by groups.
Based on the filtering rules, we define the necessary bots, and the rest will automatically go to the Honeypots section.
GUEST ACCESS
You can differentiate access for pen testers: they will not be able to see all bots in the group, but only those they need to work with.
For this purpose, it is possible to create guest accounts.
These accounts have access to specific bots, which are assigned manually by the operator.
MASS EXECUTION OF COMMANDS
There is a mechanism for bulk execution of actions for multiple bots at the same time:
Multiple bots selected in a registry can be given the same command or moved to another registry.
This reduces the number of manual actions required when executing commands of the same type.
REPORTS.
VALUE REPORT
Summary of useful bots, used to evaluate activity by selected groups and dates.
There is an upload to text format.
APPENDIX
RECOMMENDED SCRIPT FOR THE SYSINFO.BAT COMMAND
@echo off
echo General Info:
systeminfo
echo.
echo My Username:
whoami
echo.
echo Network Neighbourghoud:
net view /all
echo.
echo Domain Neighbourghoud:
net view /all /domain
echo.
echo Domain Trust:
nltest /domain_trusts /all_trusts
echo.
echo Installed Programs:
reg query hklm\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Uninstall /v
"DisplayName" /s
echo.
echo Installed Programs (wow64):
reg query hklm\SOFTWARE\Wow6432Node\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Uninstall /v
"DisplayName" /s
echo.
echo Installed Programs (current user):
reg query hkcu\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Uninstall /v
"DisplayName" /s
echo.
echo Installed Programs (current user, wow64):
reg query hkcu\SOFTWARE\Wow6432Node\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Uninstall /v
"DisplayName" /s
echo.
echo Process List:
tasklist
